does: This paper investigates whether off-policy experience from a weaker model can help a stronger model reach higher capability through reinforcement learning than standard on-policy methods alone. The authors use a smaller, domain-trained model (Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B) to generate mathematically incorrect drafts that are deliberately mismatched to the current problem being solved by a larger learner (Mathstral-7B). These wrong, mismatched drafts are injected into the context during GRPO training on mathematical problems. Testing on MATH-500 and out-of-distribution AIME benchmarks, the mismatched-wrong variant consistently outperforms standard on-policy GRPO and ablations, with mismatch providing a statistically significant +1.62 percentage point improvement over matched-wrong variants (p=0.0015 across 10 seeds). The approach shows gains on both greedy decoding and sampling-based evaluation metrics.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in using process rewards and intermediate outputs from weaker models to guide stronger learner training, extending beyond the traditional RL paradigm of learning from self-generated trajectories. It responds to limitations of purely on-policy methods like GRPO and related approaches, which struggle when models lack sufficient exploration. The paper relates to prior work on using wrong or diverse outputs as learning signals, contrastive learning in language models, and curriculum learning approaches that benefit from heterogeneous supervision. The use of mathematical domain expertise from a specialized weaker model connects to lines of research on leveraging different model scales and specializations for knowledge transfer, and builds on understanding that diversity in training signals can improve generalization and out-of-distribution performance.
enables: This finding opens new directions for efficiently scaling model capabilities by strategically using weaker but specialized models as auxiliary learning signals. It suggests weak-to-strong transfer need not require correct outputs and may benefit from deliberate distribution mismatch, pointing toward methods that could reduce reliance on expensive human feedback or self-improvement loops. The work enables future research into understanding why wrong demonstrations help—whether through contrastive learning, gradient flow properties, or exploration benefits—and whether this pattern holds across domains beyond mathematics. It could lead to practical training procedures combining multiple models at different scales, curriculum designs that strategically introduce mismatched signals, and new interpretations of how language models learn from imperfect supervision in few-shot or zero-shot regimes.
